text.title: Prompt Patterns for Common Dev Tasks Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the prompt-patterns-for-common-dev-tasks methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Every prompt opens with the pattern name on its own line (e.g. '# Refactor', '# Bug-Repro').

rationale.source: Anthropic prompting guide; internal — prompt-library audit.

rule.rationale: Naming the pattern anchors the agent and the human reviewer on the expected shape of the answer.

rule.statement: Slot 1: a one-sentence Intent statement ('I want X so that Y').
rule.rationale: Without an explicit intent the agent guesses; guesses leak into the diff.

rule.statement: Slot 2: Inputs — paths, snippets, or links the agent must read. Free-form prose inputs are rejected.
rule.rationale: Untyped inputs invite hallucination; explicit file paths anchor the agent to ground truth.

rule.statement: Slot 3: Format — the exact shape of the expected output (unified diff, JSON, test file, markdown table).
rule.rationale: Format-less prompts produce mixed-shape answers that humans must reshape before use.

rule.statement: Slot 4: Success criterion — a binary check (test passes, regex matches, count == N).
rule.rationale: Without a binary check the agent declares success by feel; the human cannot disprove it.

rule.statement: Each prompt scores 0-3 (one point per filled slot beyond the pattern name). Prompts scoring <2 are not sent.
rule.rationale: Sub-2 prompts have a 4× higher rate of needing a follow-up; gating saves round-trips.

rule.statement: If the developer uses only ghost-text completion OR the task is creative exploration with no contract, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Forcing the rubric on creative exploration kills the use case.
